<!-- prettier-ignore-start -->

# TS9003

**Category:** Typeclass Resolution · **Severity:** Error

## Message

```
@impl/@instance requires explicit type annotation (e.g., Typeclass<Type>)
```

## Explanation

The @impl/@instance macro needs to know both the typeclass and the type.

Preferred JSDoc syntax:
  /** @impl Show&lt;Point&gt; */
  const showPoint: Show&lt;Point&gt; = { ... };

The JSDoc comment specifies the typeclass and type directly.

Legacy decorator syntax:
  @impl("Show&lt;Point&gt;")
  const showPoint: Show&lt;Point&gt; = { ... };

Incorrect:
  /** @impl */
  const showPoint = { ... };  // Missing type in JSDoc or annotation

<!-- prettier-ignore-end -->
<!-- generated:end -->

## Example

```typescript
interface Point {
  x: number;
  y: number;
}

/** @impl */
// error: TS9003 — @impl/@instance requires explicit type annotation (e.g., Typeclass<Type>)
const showPoint = {
  show: (p: Point) => `(${p.x}, ${p.y})`,
};
```

## How to fix

The macro registers the instance under a `(typeclass, type)` pair, and it cannot infer that pair from a bare object literal. Name both in the JSDoc tag and annotate the `const`:

```typescript
import type { Show } from "@typesugar/std";

interface Point {
  x: number;
  y: number;
}

/** @impl Show<Point> */
const showPoint: Show<Point> = {
  show: (p) => `(${p.x}, ${p.y})`,
};
```

The legacy decorator form `@impl("Show<Point>")` carries the same information as a string argument. Either way, the `: Show<Point>` annotation also gives you type checking on the instance body itself.
